title: BLoC API Integration: Fetch Data from REST APIs

meta.title: Flutter BLoC API Integration – Complete Guide with Dio, Pagination & Error Handling (2026)
meta.description: Build Flutter apps using BLoC with REST APIs. Learn Dio integration, manage loading and error states, and implement pagination with clean architecture.

content.value: 🚀 **Quick Start (Copy‑Paste)** – If you just want a working BLoC API call right now, copy the complete code from the **Try Editor** at the bottom of this page. It includes a full example with `http`, `flutter_bloc`, and `Equatable`. For production apps, read the entire guide.

📌 **Summary (Featured Snippet)** – BLoC API integration in Flutter helps manage REST API calls using a reactive pattern with loading, success, and error states, improving scalability and testability. This guide covers Dio, pagination, clean architecture, and production‑ready code.
bloc-api-flow.png
content.alt: Flutter BLoC API integration flow diagram – events trigger bloc, repository fetches data, states update UI
content.caption: Figure 1: How BLoC handles API calls – from user action to UI update.

content.value: In real-world Flutter apps, fetching data from APIs is unavoidable. Without proper state management, handling loading, errors, and pagination becomes messy. This guide shows how to use BLoC to build scalable, production-ready API integrations with clean architecture. Whether you need a simple **Flutter REST API example** or a full **BLoC API tutorial**, you'll find everything here.
Why Use BLoC for API Calls?

value: **Separation of concerns** – Business logic stays out of the UI.
**Reactive state** – UI automatically rebuilds when data arrives.
**Testability** – Blocs and repositories can be tested without the UI.
**Error handling** – Dedicated error states provide user feedback.
**Loading indicators** – Built‑in loading states improve UX.

content.value: For a deeper understanding of BLoC architecture, check our guide on BLoC Clean Architecture and BLoC Error Handling.
Project Setup
Add the necessary dependencies to your `pubspec.yaml`:

dependencies:
  flutter:
    sdk: flutter
  flutter_bloc: ^8.1.5
  equatable: ^2.0.5
  http: ^1.2.0          # or dio: ^5.4.0
  get_it: ^7.6.7        # optional for DI
  
dev_dependencies:
  bloc_test: ^9.1.5
  mockito: ^5.4.3
Run `flutter pub get`.
Data Model
Create a model class for the data you're fetching. We'll use JSONPlaceholder's posts endpoint as an example.

content.title: models/post.dart

content.value: States
Define the states your bloc can be in. For a typical fetch, you'll have loading, success, and error states.

content.title: bloc/post_state.dart

content.value: Events
Define the events that trigger state changes. For a simple fetch, we need an event to load posts.

content.title: bloc/post_event.dart

content.value: Repository
The repository is responsible for fetching data from the API. It abstracts the data source and can be easily mocked in tests. Learn more about the Flutter HTTP client and Dio setup.

content.title: repositories/post_repository.dart

content.value: Bloc Implementation
Now combine the repository with events and states to create the bloc.

content.title: bloc/post_bloc.dart

content.value: Dependency Injection (Optional)
Use `get_it` to register dependencies and make them available throughout the app. This helps with testing and keeps the code clean.

content.title: di/injection.dart

content.value: UI Integration
Provide the bloc to the widget tree using `BlocProvider` and listen to states with `BlocBuilder`/`BlocConsumer`.

content.title: pages/post_page.dart

content.value: Using Dio Instead of http
Dio is a more powerful HTTP client with interceptors, cancel tokens, and better error handling. Here's how to adapt the repository to use Dio.

content.title: repositories/post_repository_dio.dart

content.value: http vs dio – Which One to Choose?
**Verdict:** Use `http` for quick prototypes or when you only need basic GET/POST. Switch to `dio` for production apps that need interceptors, cancel tokens, or advanced error handling.
Pagination Example
For infinite scrolling, extend the state to include pagination data and add a `LoadMore` event.
Real-World Use Cases

value: **Social media feeds** – Endless scrolling with pagination and pull‑to‑refresh.
**E‑commerce product listings** – Filtering, sorting, and caching.
**Chat applications** – Real‑time message updates with WebSockets + BLoC.
**News aggregators** – Offline support and background sync.

content.value: When NOT to Use BLoC

value: ❌ **Very small apps** (e.g., 2–3 screens with minimal state) – Use `setState` or a simple `ValueNotifier`.
❌ **One‑time API calls with no user interaction** – A `FutureBuilder` is enough.
❌ **Apps with no complex state transitions** – Don't over‑engineer.
✅ **When you need testability, predictability, and a clear separation of concerns** – That's where BLoC shines.

content.value: Performance Tips

value: ⚡ **Cache API responses** – Use a local database (Hive, SQLite) to avoid redundant network calls.
⚡ **Avoid unnecessary rebuilds** – Always extend `Equatable` in states and events.
⚡ **Use `BlocBuilder` with `buildWhen`** – Rebuild only when specific properties change.
⚡ **Implement pagination wisely** – Load more only when the user scrolls near the bottom.
⚡ **Cancel pending requests** – When using Dio, cancel requests if the screen is disposed to avoid memory leaks.

content.value: Best Practices

value: **Use repositories** – Keep network logic out of blocs.
**Define explicit states** – Loading, success, error, and optionally empty states.
**Handle errors gracefully** – Show user‑friendly messages and provide retry options.
**Use `Equatable`** – Prevents unnecessary rebuilds.
**Test your blocs** – Use `bloc_test` to verify state changes.
**Use a service locator like `get_it`** – Centralises dependency creation.
**Cancel pending requests** – When using Dio, cancel requests if the screen is disposed to avoid memory leaks.

content.value: Common Mistakes

value:
❌ **Calling API directly inside the bloc** – Hard to test and change.
✅ Use a repository.

❌ **Not handling network errors** – App may crash.
✅ Catch exceptions and emit error states.

❌ **Not using `Equatable`** – Causes unnecessary UI rebuilds.
✅ Extend `Equatable` in states and events.

❌ **Blocking the UI with synchronous code** – Network calls should be async.
✅ Always use `Future` and `async`.

❌ **Forgetting to dispose stream subscriptions** – Memory leaks.
✅ BlocProvider handles disposal automatically.

content.value: Conclusion

Integrating APIs with BLoC gives you a clean, reactive architecture. By separating network logic into repositories and defining explicit states, you build apps that are robust, testable, and user‑friendly. Whether you use `http` or `dio`, the pattern remains the same: events trigger state changes, and the UI reacts accordingly. For more advanced topics, explore our BLoC Clean Architecture and error handling guides.

faq.question: Should I use http or dio with BLoC?
faq.answer: Both work. `dio` offers more features like interceptors, cancel tokens, and form data, which are useful for larger apps. `http` is simpler and fine for basic needs. See the comparison table above for details.

faq.question: How do I handle API token authentication?
faq.answer: Store the token (e.g., in `get_it` as a singleton) and add it to the headers in the repository. You can use `dio` interceptors to add it automatically.

faq.question: How do I test the API integration?
faq.answer: Mock the repository in bloc tests. Use `mockito` or `mocktail` to return fake responses. You can also test the repository with `http.Client` using `MockClient` from the `http/testing` package.

faq.question: How to handle slow network or timeout?
faq.answer: Set timeouts on the HTTP client (e.g., `dio.options.connectTimeout`). In the bloc, catch timeout exceptions and emit an appropriate error state.

faq.question: Can I use BLoC with GraphQL?
faq.answer: Yes, the same pattern applies. The repository would use a GraphQL client instead of REST, but the bloc remains unchanged.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a repository in BLoC API integration?
options: To abstract data fetching logic and make it testable

quiz.question: Which state should you emit when an API request fails?
Why is `Equatable` recommended for states and events?
options: It prevents unnecessary UI rebuilds by enabling value-based equality
